coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on porphyrin-based MOF for phosphate ions detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanfeng
Ren, Baoping
Dong, Wenyue
Duan, Qian
Fei, Teng
coredata.dc:description: Porphyrin-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a type of periodic network framework materials constructed from porphyrin or metalloporphyrin as the structural unit with metal ions or metal clusters. Due to their unique photoelectric property, porphyrin-based MOFs have potential application in fluorescence (FL) sensing. In this study, we have successfully prepared porphyrin-based MOF (Zn-TCPP(BPDC)) using the hydrothermal method, based on 5,10,15,20-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP) as first ligand, 4,4′-biphenyldicarboxylic acid (BPDC) as second ligand and Zn2+ as the metal node. The structure, morphology and porosity of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and N2 adsorption isotherms. Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits a sheet-like structure with a side length of ca. 1 μm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area is 98.5 m2/g, and the pore size is mainly concentrated at 2.5 nm. The porosity could provide channels for the adsorption and diffusion of analytes, which helps to improve sensing performance. Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits high sensitivity and low detection limit towards phosphate ions (Pi), which is attributed to strong coordination of phosphate to Zn2+ that leads to the collapse of the layered structure of Zn-TCPP(BPDC), resulting in the release of TCPP monomers and enhanced FL emission. Additionally, FL sensing with single-wavelength is susceptible to environmental interference, which reduces the reliability. To address this, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced into the sensing system of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution to provide a reference FL signal, enabling visualization of the ratiometric FL sensing for Pi in aqueous solution with a low detection limit (0.13 μM), excellent anti-interference and selectivity. Finally, a portable sensing platform was constructed using a smart phone color recognizer, enabling real-time and visual ratiometric FL sensing of Pi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porphyrin
Metal–organic frameworks
Fluorescent sensing
Ratiometric fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photophysical properties of CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC)
Ratiometric FL sensing of Pi based on CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C 1s, (c) Zn 2p and (d) N 1s XPS spectra of Zn-TCPP(BPDC). (e) FT-IR spectra of TCPP, Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and CDs; (f) FT-IR spectra of Pi, CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) + Pi.
SEM images of (a) Zn-TCPP(BPDC), (b) CDs, (c) CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and (d) CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) + Pi; (e) nitrogen isothermal adsorption–desorption curve; (f) pore size distribution of Zn-TCPP(BPDC).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) FL spectra of CDs in aqueous solution before and after Pi addition; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra and (d) FL spectra of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) in aqueous solution before and after Pi addition.
(a) FL spectra of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) at different Pi concentrations; (b) linear fit plot of F608/F455 values of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) vs. Pi concentration.
Linear plot of color change (R/B) of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) solution vs. Pi concentration; the inset shows the photographs irradiated with a UV lamp at 365 nm.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on porphyrin-based MOF for phosphate ions detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porphyrin-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a type of periodic network framework materials constructed from porphyrin or metalloporphyrin as the structural unit with metal ions or metal clusters. Due to their unique photoelectric property, porphyrin-based MOFs have potential application in fluorescence (FL) sensing. In this study, we have successfully prepared porphyrin-based MOF (Zn-TCPP(BPDC)) using the hydrothermal method, based on 5,10,15,20-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP) as first ligand, 4,4′-biphenyldicarboxylic acid (BPDC) as second ligand and Zn2+ as the metal node. The structure, morphology and porosity of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and N2 adsorption isotherms. Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits a sheet-like structure with a side length of ca. 1 μm. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area is 98.5 m2/g, and the pore size is mainly concentrated at 2.5 nm. The porosity could provide channels for the adsorption and diffusion of analytes, which helps to improve sensing performance. Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits high sensitivity and low detection limit towards phosphate ions (Pi), which is attributed to strong coordination of phosphate to Zn2+ that leads to the collapse of the layered structure of Zn-TCPP(BPDC), resulting in the release of TCPP monomers and enhanced FL emission. Additionally, FL sensing with single-wavelength is susceptible to environmental interference, which reduces the reliability. To address this, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced into the sensing system of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution to provide a reference FL signal, enabling visualization of the ratiometric FL sensing for Pi in aqueous solution with a low detection limit (0.13 μM), excellent anti-interference and selectivity. Finally, a portable sensing platform was constructed using a smart phone color recognizer, enabling real-time and visual ratiometric FL sensing of Pi.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphate ions (Pi) plays an important role in the water environment as an essential nutrient that promotes the growth of aquatic plants and animals. However, excessive Pi can cause water eutrophication, resulting in a large number of algae and aquatic plants in the water that consume dissolved oxygen, deteriorating the water quality, and making the animals in the water die due to the hypoxia [1]. Eventually, the biodiversity may decline and the species composition may change. In addition, excessive Pi will also cause soil compaction, alkalization and desertification, and in serious cases will cause the soil to lose the necessary conditions to provide crop growth. Moreover, Pi also plays a crucial role in matter exchange and energy transfer within biological systems [2]. Many diseases are related to Pi content, for example, the lack of Pi in the human body would cause hypophosphatemia, and hyperphosphatemia is a disease when the concentration of phosphates in the serum is greater than 1.46 mM [3]; long-term intake of large amounts of Pi can also lead to diseases such as kidney disease [4]. The World Health Organization recommends that the maximum allowable Pi concentration in drinking water is 0.03 mM [5]. Therefore, the efficient and sensitive detection of Pi is of great significance to human health and environmental protection.

Recently, researchers have developed various analytical methods to detect Pi, such as surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6], energy dispersive X-ray diffraction [7], nuclear magnetic resonance [8], ion mobility spectrometry [9], electrochemical method [10], and so on. However, these methods are often costly, require complex operations, or lack portability, greatly limiting their practical applications. Consequently, researchers have turned their attention to fluorescence (FL) sensing method, due to the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, good accuracy, and high selectivity [11–16]. Materials used for FL sensing include quantum dots [17–20], covalent organic frameworks [21–23], conjugated microporous polymers [24–27], and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [28–30], etc. MOFs, in particular, are widely used in gas adsorption and separation [31], catalysis [32–34], treatment [35], and FL sensing [36] because of their high porosity, large specific surface area, and diverse structures [37–39]. Several MOF-based FL probes have been designed for Pi detection [40,41]. The strong coordination of phosphate to metal cations could lead to the collapse of the structure of MOFs, resulting in the release of molecular ligands and enhanced FL emission, thus achieving the Pi detection. However, single-modal probes are prone to uncertainties due to probe concentration, emission and excitation intensity, and/or environmental interferences. Dual-emission probes could effectively dissolve the above problems by constructing ratiometric FL sensing. For example, Yin et al. prepared a dual-emission MOF named Eu-DATA/BDC, which realized the ratiometric sensing of Cu2+ ions [42]. Xia et al. prepared several MOFs with strong dual emission for the detection of Pi and adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) by ratiometric FL [43].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in the field of FL sensing due to their excellent optical property [44,45]. Based on the flexible tunability of the FL of CDs, the dual-emission with high contrast can be realized by a simple method of combination of CDs and MOF for ratiometric FL sensing [46,47]. In this study, porphyrin-based MOF named Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was designed and synthesized via hydrothermal method, using Zn2+ as the metal node, 5,10,15,20-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP) and 4,4′-biphenyldicarboxylic acid (BPDC) as the first and second ligands in this study, respectively. Based on this, CDs were introduced into Zn-TCPP(BPDC) to construct a ratiometric FL sensing system, which shows a low detection limit of 0.13 μM and excellent anti-interference characteristic and selectivity towards Pi detection. Finally, a portable sensing visualization platform was developed based on smart phone, which can quickly and easily visualize the Pi sensing.
Details for materials and equipment are listed in the Supplementary material.

The synthetic route of CDs is shown in Scheme S1(a). Citric acid (CA) (1.26 g, 6 mmol) and urea (UA) (1.08 g, 18 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a reaction vessel and heated at 160 °C in an oven for 8 h. After the reaction was cooled down to room temperature, the desired product was obtained by filtration through microporous filter (0.22 μm) to remove the insoluble substances, and then dialyzed with deionized water (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the resulting solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

TCPP was prepared according to our previous reported literature [48]. The porphyrin-based MOF Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was prepared using a solvothermal method, as shown in Scheme S1(b). Zn (OAc)2·2H2O (5 mg, 0.017 mmol), TCPP (4 mg, 0.005 mmol), and BPDC (4 mg, 0.017 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF by ultrasonication for 10 min, and then heated at 150 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature and washed with deionized water. The product was collected by centrifugation as purple powder.

The CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) composite material was obtained by dissolving CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC) in 100 mL deionized water with different concentrations (as illustrated in Sections 3.3 and 3.4), and followed by ultrasonication for 30 min.

The crystallinity and phase composition of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) were obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement, as shown in Fig. S1. The XRD pattern reveals the presence of three distinct peaks at 6.1o, 7.6o and 18.2o, corresponding to the (100), (110) and (004) lattice planes of Zn-TCPP(BPDC), respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was performed. Fig. 1 (a) reveals the presence of C, N, O and Zn in Zn-TCPP(BPDC). Furthermore, an in-depth analysis of the chemical states of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was conducted using high-resolution XPS. The C 1s XPS spectrum exhibits five distinct peaks at 284.8, 286.3, 288.5, 290.8 and 292.2 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–N, C=N, C–O/C=O and π–π*, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The Zn 2p XPS presents two main peaks at 1021.4 and 1044.4 eV, which are attributed to Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(c). For N 1s XPS, it exhibits three distinct peaks at 398.2, 400.2 and 401.3 eV, representing N–Zn, C=N and C–N, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(d).

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of TCPP, Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and CDs are shown in Fig. 1(e). Compared with TCPP, the stretching vibration absorption of C=O shifts from 1700 cm−1 to 1680 cm−1 due to the formation of coordination bonds in Zn-TCPP(BPDC), indicating the successful preparation of Zn-TCPP(BPDC). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 3150 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, respectively, the peak at 1700 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of C=O stretching vibration, the peak at 1400 cm−1 is the bending vibration of N–H, and the peak at 1200 cm−1 is the absorption peak of C–O stretching vibration. The above data indicate the successful preparation of CDs, with the presence of hydrophilic groups such as –OH, −COOH, and–NH2 on the surface.

To investigate the interaction between CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) complex and the analyte Pi, the FT-IR spectra of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) before and after adding Pi were tested, as shown in Fig. 1(f). It can be seen that CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits characteristic absorption peaks of both CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC). After adding Pi, a characteristic absorption peak of Pi appears in the range of 1200–900 cm−1, which shows noticeable red shift, attributed to the binding of Zn2+ to the P–O bond that restricts the stretching vibration [5,41] and reduces the vibration frequency, thus indicating the complexation between Pi and Zn2+. Furthermore, after interacting with Pi, the absorption peak of carboxyl groups in CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was weakened and slightly blue-shifted, indicating that Pi weakened the coordination between TCPP ligands and Zn2+ [49].

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) is shown in Fig. 2 (a), which displays a square two-dimensional sheet structure with uniform size of approximately 1 μm in length. Fig. 2(b) shows the SEM image of CDs, displaying uniform nanoscale particles concentrated at 10 nm. The small sizes are beneficial for dispersion in aqueous phase. In Fig. 2(c), for CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC), Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and CDs are uniformly mixed together without significant changes in morphology compared to the individual components, indicating a simple physical blending occurred. In Fig. 2(d), the SEM image shows the interaction between CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) and Pi, where the original regular sheet structure of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) is clearly disrupted, and the irregular scattered structure occurs, indicating that Pi is complexed with Zn2+ in Zn-TCPP(BPDC) due to the high affinity between them and destroy the framework structure of the MOF. Due to the significant difference in size between CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC), CDs might be covered by the scattered structure of the disrupted Zn-TCPP(BPDC) at the magnification level in Fig. 2(d), hence no obvious distribution of CDs is observed.

Fig. 2(e) shows the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of Zn-TCPP(BPDC). Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits the characteristics of a type III adsorption isotherm. The specific surface area is 98.5 m2/g. Fig. 2(f) shows that the pore size of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) is primarily concentrated at 2.5 nm. The high specific surface area and porous nature of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) are advantageous for the adsorption and diffusion of the target substance Pi, which is beneficial to improve the FL sensing performance for Pi [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC)
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of CDs with and without Pi are shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). CDs display a clear absorption peak at 340 nm, and there is no significant change in the UV–vis absorption peak after the addition of Pi (Fig. 3(a)), indicating that the addition of Pi does not alter the structure of CDs and has no effect on its absorption property. In Fig. 3(b), the FL spectrum of CDs shows a distinct FL emission peak at around 450 nm, emitting blue FL, and there is no significant change in FL intensity after the addition of Pi with only a slightly red-shift, indicating that the addition of Pi has no significant effect on the FL emission of CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution before and after the addition of Pi are shown in Fig. 3(c). The absorption intensity of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution is weak, but it is significantly enhanced after the addition of Pi, showing a clear absorption from TCPP monomers. As shown in Fig. 3(d), Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution has a relatively weak FL emission peak at 608 nm; after the addition of Pi, the FL of the sensing system is dramatically enhanced, which could be attributed to the coordination competition between the phosphate group and TCPP with Zn2+, ultimately leading to the collapse of the Zn-TCPP(BPDC) framework and releasing TCPP molecules, thereby enhancing the FL emission of TCPP at 608 nm. The inset in Fig. 3(d) visualizes the effect of Pi on the FL intensity of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution, showing that the addition of Pi significantly enhances the red emission of the sensing system.

Based on the above analysis, the CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) composite solution ([CDs] = 10 μg/mL, [Zn-TCPP(BPDC)] = 80 μg/mL) was adopted for the Pi sensing. As shown in Fig. S2, the CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) exhibits two distinct FL emission peaks at 455 nm and 608 nm, corresponding to the emissions of CDs and Zn-TCPP(BPDC), respectively. Upon the Pi addition, the emission peak of CDs at 455 nm remains unaffected, while the emission intensity of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) at 608 nm significantly increases, which is consistent with the above analysis. Here, the blue emission of CDs serves as a reference, and the enhanced red emission resulting from the interaction between phosphate and Zn-TCPP(BPDC) serves as a detection FL signal, together achieving the ratiometric FL sensing of Pi.

The ratio of CDs to Zn-TCPP(BPDC), pH level, and the incubation time are important factors that affect the sensing performance of the FL probe system. In order to achieve the ideal sensing performance, the optimization experiments on these three factors were conducted. First, the influence of different pH values on the FL of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) solution was tested. The pH was adjusted using 4-hydroxyethylpiperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer solution. As shown in Fig. S3(a), it can be observed that the addition of Pi has a significant impact on the relative FL intensity F608/F455 of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) under different pH values. In Fig. S3(b), the trend of Δ(F608/F455) values as pH changes are recorded. It can be seen that when pH = 5–7, the Δ(F608/F455) value increases with the increase of pH. Under acidic condition, the lower the pH, the higher the degree of phosphate protonation, thus leading to a weakening of the coordination ability. When pH = 7, the value of Δ(F608/F455) reaches its maximum. Within the pH range of 7–9, the Δ(F608/F455) value decreases as the pH increases, which might be attributed to the not very stable structure of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) in alkaline environment, causing some TCPP molecules to be released from the framework, thus resulting in enhanced background signal. Therefore, pH = 7 was determined as the optimal pH value for the FL probe system.

The ratio of CDs to Zn-TCPP(BPDC) of the FL probe also affects the performance of the sensing system. As shown in Fig. S3(c), by fixing the concentration of CDs at 10 μg/mL and changing the concentration of Zn-TCPP(BPDC), the optimal ratio of CDs to Zn-TCPP(BPDC) in the FL probe system was investigated. In the range of 0–78 μg/mL, the F608/F455 value of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) gradually increases as the concentration of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) increases. When the concentration of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) is 78 μg/mL, the F608/F455 reaches the maximum value. Continuing to increase the concentration to 115 μg/mL, the F608/F455 value slightly decreases. Therefore, the optimal concentration of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) is determined to be 78 μg/mL. Fig. S3(d) records the relationship between F608/F455 and the incubation time. With incubation time increasing from 1 min to 9 min, the F608/F455 value continuously increases. Further increasing the incubation time does not result in a significant change in the F608/F455 value, indicating that the optimal incubation effect is achieved at 9 min. Therefore, the incubation time is determined to be 9 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL sensing of Pi based on CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC)
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the ratiometric FL sensing of Pi based on CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was carried out, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that with the increase of Pi concentration, the FL intensity at 608 nm belonging to TCPP gradually increases, while the FL intensity of CDs at 455 nm remains almost unchanged (Fig. 4(a)). The F608/F455 value shows a good linear relationship with Pi concentration in the range of 0–131.25 μM (Fig. 4(b)), with a linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9981 and a slope of 3.35 × 104 M−1. As important sensing parameters, the LOD (Limit of Detection) and LOQ (Limit of Quantitation) were determined according to the formulae LOD = 3σ/Slope and LOQ = 10σ/Slope, where σ represents the standard deviation of blank sample. The LOD was calculated as 0.13 μM and the LOQ was calculated as 0.45 μM (Table S1), both of which are lower than the maximum allowable Pi concentration in drinking water [5]. The sensing performance of Pi based on the single turn-on FL signal of Zn-TCPP(BPDC) was conducted as comparison, which indicates relatively high LOD and LOQ of 0.56 μM and 1.87 μM, respectively (Fig. S4, Table S1), demonstrating the advantage of ratiometric FL sensing based on CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC).

There are various metal cations, halides and oxyanions in the actual water environment, so it is very important to explore the anti-interference performance and selectivity of the sensing system. The common ions including
N a
+
,
C a
2 +
,
K
+
,
C u
2 +
,
F e
3 +
,
M g
2 +
,
C l
-
,
I
-
,
B r
-
,
N O
3
-
,
S O
4
2 -
,
A c
-
and
C O
3
2 -
were selected for the selectivity and anti-interference tests. As shown in Fig. S5, upon the addition of 50 μM interfering ions, the F608/F455 value of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) remains almost constant, which increases significantly when 80 μM Pi is added to the above solution, with comparable value to that of Pi detection alone. The result indicates that the ratiometric FL sensing system of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) has high selectivity and good anti-interference performance for aqueous Pi detection.

Based on the high sensitivity and selectivity of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) for ratiometric FL sensing towards Pi in aqueous solution, the detection of Pi in actual water samples was tested via the standard spike/recovery method. The standard spike equation is y = 0.0335x + 0.2382 (R2 = 0.9981) (Fig. 4(b)). Three types of water samples were chosen, including deionized water, lake water (Changchun Nanhu Lake) and tap water (laboratory), and the spike amount of Pi is 20.00 μM. As shown in Table 1 , it can be seen that the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for Pi detection by CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) in the three water samples are between 1.79 % and 2.11 %, with recoveries ranging from 97.75 % to 102.20 %. In order to evaluate the above-mentioned spiked/recovery data, the t-test method is adopted for validation, and the t-test data are shown in Table S2. It can be seen that the calculated t exp. in the three water samples are 2.36, 2.33 and 2.54, respectively, which are all less than the t ref. (4.30), indicating that there is no significant difference between the detection result of the ratiometric FL sensing method and the actual spike amount under 95% confidence level, demonstrating the accurate detection capacity of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) towards Pi in actual water samples.

Based on the ratiometric FL sensing signals of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) for Pi detection, the visual sensing platform was constructed, as shown in Fig. S6. The “Color Recognizer” app of the smartphone was used to read the RGB ratio (R/B), and a standard equation is established based on the R/B value and Pi concentration to construct the visual sensing platform as shown in Fig. 5 . The R/B value of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) shows a good linear relationship with Pi concentration in the range of 15.0–150.0 μM, with a slope of 1.14 × 104 M−1 and R2 of 0.9930. The LOD was determined to be 1.45 μM and LOQ was determined to be 4.82 μM, respectively (σ = 0.0055). In order to visually observe the fluorescence change, the ratiometric FL sensing photos were taken under 365 nm UV illumination, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5. With the increase of Pi concentration, the color of CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) aqueous solution changes from blue to sky blue to light purple to pale pink to dark pink to deep red, enabling the visual detection of Pi in water environment.

In summary, the CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) composite was successfully constructed and the highly efficient ratiometric FL sensing towards Pi in aqueous solution was achieved with LOD as low as 0.13 μM. Moreover, the CDs/Zn-TCPP(BPDC) ratiometric FL probe system has good selectivity and anti-interference performance against common ions. In addition, the standard spike/recovery test indicates the recoveries of Pi are between 97.75 % and 102.20 % in actual water samples, and the accuracy is verified by t-test. Finally, a portable visual sensing platform for detecting Pi in water environment was developed, and the probe shows a significant FL change (blue to pink and finally to red) with the increase of Pi concentration. This low-cost and easy-to-use method enables high sensitivity and visual sensing of Pi in the environmental water samples.

Yuanfeng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Baoping Ren: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenyue Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qian Duan: Writing – review & editing. Teng Fei: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China [CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0245], and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [51803013].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical formation of molybdenum phosphate on a pencil graphite electrode and its potential application for the detection of phosphate ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.B. Arvas, H. Gürsu, M. Gençten, Y. Sahin, Electrochemical formation of molybdenum phosphate on a pencil graphite electrode and its potential application for the detection of phosphate ions, Anal. Methods 10 (2018) 4282–4291.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Displacement assay detection by a dimeric lanthanide luminescent ternary Tb(Ⅲ)-cyclen complex: high selectivity for phosphate and nitrate anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.F. Caffrey, T. Gunnlaugsson, Displacement assay detection by a dimeric lanthanide luminescent ternary Tb(Ⅲ)-cyclen complex: High selectivity for phosphate and nitrate anions, Dalton Trans. 43 (2014) 17964–17970.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.A. Block, P.S. Klassen, J.M. Lazarus, N. Ofsthun, E.G. Lowrie, G.M. Chertow, Mineral metabolism, mortality, and morbidity in maintenance hemodialysis, J. Am. Soc. Nephrol. 15 (2004) 2208–2218.
